metadata.title: Defence Spending as Share of GDP — G7 + Peers
metadata.description: Military expenditure as percentage of GDP for G7 countries, using SIPRI data as primary source with NATO cross-checks where available

primarySource.name: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database
primarySource.notes: SIPRI provides the most comprehensive, consistent, and internationally comparable dataset on military expenditure. Covers 1949-2024 for most countries. Uses a common definition of military spending across all countries including non-NATO members (Japan). Data is sourced from official government budgets, defence white papers, and international financial statistics.

crossCheckSource.name: NATO Defence Expenditure Reports
crossCheckSource.notes: NATO publishes annual defence expenditure data for member states using its own definition. NATO data covers 2014-2025 for members only (excludes Japan). Each Ally's Ministry of Defence reports expenditure according to agreed NATO definition.

crossCheckNotes.sipriVsNato: SIPRI and NATO figures differ slightly because they use different definitions of military spending. NATO's definition includes military pensions and some paramilitary spending that SIPRI may exclude, and SIPRI includes some items NATO does not. For the UK, NATO figures tend to be 0.05-0.15pp higher than SIPRI. For example, UK 2024: NATO reports 2.33%, SIPRI reports ~2.3%. We use SIPRI as primary because: (1) it covers all G7 including Japan, (2) it provides longer time series (back to 2000), (3) it uses a single consistent methodology across all countries.
crossCheckNotes.sipriVsWorldBank: World Bank military expenditure data is sourced directly from SIPRI. They are the same dataset. World Bank indicator MS.MIL.XPND.GD.ZS mirrors SIPRI figures.
crossCheckNotes.natoDefinition: NATO defines defence expenditure as payments by national government specifically to meet armed forces needs. Includes military pensions, civil defence, certain paramilitary forces. Does NOT include civil defence if not part of military, or foreign aid. NATO switched from older (1953) to current definition around 2004.
crossCheckNotes.sipriDefinition: SIPRI defines military expenditure as current and capital expenditure on armed forces (including peacekeeping), defence ministries, paramilitary forces (if judged trained/equipped for military operations), and military space activities. Includes military pensions and personnel costs.
crossCheckNotes.japanNote: Japan is not a NATO member so NATO data is unavailable. SIPRI is the only standardised source for Japan. Japan's spending was historically constrained by an informal 1% of GDP ceiling, which was lifted in the 2022 National Security Strategy (target: 2% by 2027).

metadata.lastReleaseNotes: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2026 edition published 27 Apr 2026 (covering 1949–2025). Headlines: world military spending $2,887bn (+2.9% real), global burden 2.5% GDP. UK -2.0% real to $89.0bn; Germany +24% to $114bn (2.3% GDP, first time >2% since 1990); Japan +9.7% to $62.2bn (1.4% GDP, highest share since 1958); France +1.5% to $68bn; Canada +23%.

defenceSpendPctGDP.description: Military expenditure as percentage of GDP for G7 countries
defenceSpendPctGDP.methodologyNote: SIPRI military expenditure as share of GDP. Calculated as military expenditure in local currency at current prices divided by nominal GDP. Figures may differ from NATO estimates by 0.05-0.15pp due to definitional differences (see crossCheckNotes). Where SIPRI 2024 data is preliminary, this is noted.

series.notes: SIPRI 2026 release: US among the few countries showing real-terms decreases in 2025 (alongside UK and Israel). Remains world's largest spender.
NATO reports 2.07-2.33% depending on revision. Ukraine effect.
SIPRI preliminary. NATO reports 2.33%. Target: 2.5% by 2027.
NATO 2025 estimate ~2.4% (SIPRI 2026 release: -2.0% real-terms to $89.0bn — UK among the few countries with falling spending). UK Treasury fiscal-year target 2.36% GDP for 2025/26.
SIPRI preliminary. NATO reports 2.06%. LPM (military programming law) ramp-up.
SIPRI 2026 release: +1.5% to $68bn — modest increase under LPM. Continues progression towards 2% NATO target.
Zeitenwende speech. EUR 100bn special fund announced.
SIPRI preliminary. NATO reports 2.12%. Crosses 2% for first time.
SIPRI 2026 release: +24% to $114bn (largest spender among European NATO). Crossed 2% threshold for first time since 1990 (already 2.1% in 2024 per NATO; SIPRI now finalises 2025 at 2.3%).
NATO reports 1.49%. Still well below 2% target.
NATO reports 1.37%. Lowest among major NATO allies.
SIPRI 2026 release: +23% real-terms in 2025 — sharpest jump in any G7 country. Pledged 2% NATO target by 2032.
SIPRI preliminary. Major jump under 5-year defence plan.
SIPRI 2026 release: +9.7% to $62.2bn — 1.4% of GDP, highest share since 1958. National Security Strategy target: 2% by 2027.

natoTarget.label: NATO 2% guideline
natoTarget.note: Agreed at 2014 Wales Summit. In June 2025, NATO raised the target to 5% of GDP.

headline.ukBudgetNote: 2025-26 MOD budget. Rising to £73.5bn by 2028-29. Target: 2.6% GDP.
headline.meetingTargetCount: All 32 NATO allies expected to meet 2% in 2025; 22 of 29 European NATO allies hit ≥2% per SIPRI 2026 release
